questions.prompt: What are the nominal and fully-charged voltages of a LiPo cell, respectively?
questions.explanation: A LiPo cell is nominally 3.7V (the value written on the label) and is charged to a maximum of 4.2V when full. Going above 4.2V is dangerous.

questions.prompt: When you finish a flight and land, what should the resting (after the load is removed) voltage of a healthy LiPo cell be roughly, and what is the absolute damage threshold?
options: ~3.7-3.8V at rest; absolute damage threshold 3.0V (never go below it)
questions.explanation: A healthy landing target is ~3.7-3.8V per cell at rest. Under load (when you pin the throttle) the voltage can momentarily sag to ~3.5V, but it recovers once the load is removed. 3.0V is NOT a flight target but the absolute damage threshold you must never go below; over-discharging below it causes permanent damage. Land when the alarm sounds.

questions.prompt: If you won't use the battery for more than a few days, at what level (storage voltage) should you store it?
questions.explanation: LiPos last longest at a storage voltage of ~3.8V per cell. Leaving them fully charged or fully empty ages them quickly and can cause swelling.

questions.prompt: To charge a 1500mAh LiPo at the safe standard rate of 1C, what current do you use?
questions.explanation: 1C means a current equal to the capacity. Since 1500mAh = 1.5Ah, the 1C charge current is 1.5A. When in doubt, using 1C is the safest.

questions.prompt: What should you do with a swollen (puffed) or crash-damaged LiPo?
options: Throw it in the regular trash
Stop flying it, secure it in a LiPo-safe bag, and take it to a special waste/recycling point (don't throw it in the trash)
Fix it by charging it at a higher current
questions.explanation: A swollen/damaged LiPo is at the end of its life and can catch fire; don't fly it and don't throw it in the regular trash. Secure it in a LiPo-safe bag and take it to a special waste/recycling point.
